feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Extended Warehouse Management
feed.subtitle: SAP Extended Warehouse Management blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: SAP Extended Warehouse Management Introduction
entry.content:
EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) manages your warehouse stocks, but to a more deeper level. In the normal inventory management process in the materials management module, quantities and stocks are managed to the storage location level. In EWM, it is processing various goods movements and stocks in more detailed level in the system to the storage bin level. You can apply physical goods movements, and you can organize, control and monitor these goods movement through the EWM system.

EWM Structure:

EWM Structure Details:

Warehouse Number: It is highest level in the warehouse structure. It can manage the entire the warehouse structure.

Storage Type: It is the individual warehouse facilities, where the products are stored.

Storage Section: The Storage types are divided into storage sections. Storage bins with common attributes belong to one storage section. It is something like a way of grouping. 

Storage Bin: It informs you the exact position of the material, where you can store your products.

Quant: It is the quantity of the materials in the storage bin. It is used for inventory management of a product in the storage bin.

There is also another terminology called Activity Area. It can refer to a storage bin, or a group of concatenated bins from several storage types. Storage bins can be assigned manually to activity areas. 

Storage Bin level the can manage all the stocks. Every storage bin is mapped in the system, where you can track all the stock movements in details. You can know exactly, where a specific product is located in the warehouse. It like having a very low level view of your stock in the warehouse from the smallest storage unit going upwards to viewing the entire warehouse. In EWM for sure you can process all the normal goods movement processes such as goods receipts, goods issues, stock transfers and automatic replenishment.

Physical Inventory in the EWM is where you compare between the actual stock existing in your warehouse with the data in the EWM system. You start by entering the physical inventory differences, and checking if there is any differences between the actual stock and the system to start monitoring your stock levels. Physical Inventory in EWM is at the Warehouse Management Level. It can be performed based on the storage bins. EWM contains a difference monitor available with tolerance groups to define maximum values for posting differences for each user. EWM gives a feature of having an overview of all goods movements and warehouse stocks.

Understanding the Organizational structure is from the fundamentals of any SAP module. Let's check more details regarding the Org. Structure of EWM:

For Warehouse Number:

You can manage warehouse number to handle several storage facilities, which together form the complete warehouse structure. It means warehouse number can be used independently for managing the entire warehouse structure.

Example of the facilities in the Warehouse structure:

● Goods receipt area
● Goods issue area
● Hall with high rack shelves
● Bulk storage area
● Activity areas of category Picking with Fixed Bins

For Storage Type:

It is considered a storage zone, which you define for the warehouse number in the EWM. It can be considered as a physical subdivision within the warehouse. It consist of one or more storage bins. 

Examples of the Physical Storage Types:

● Bulk storage area
● General storage area
● High rack storage area
● Fixed bin storage area
● Rack storage area

For Storage Section:

It is a subdivision of storage type, which group together storage bins with similar attributes for the putaway process. For high rack storage area, there are two types of bins low bins and high bins. It can be divided into two storage sections for slow and fast moving items.

For Storage Bin:

It is the smallest unit in the warehouse, which represents the exact position where the product can be located in the warehouse. There are attributes in the warehouse for the storage bin such as maximum weight, capacity and storage bin type of small or large pallets.

For Quant:

System manages different batches of a product as quants. Quantity can be increased and decreased through goods movements. Quantity of a quant can increase by adding stock to the existing stock. It can only be created and deleted using movements.

Quant record consists of:

● Quant identification
● Number
● Batch number
● Stock category type
● Stock category
● Stock usage

For Activity Area:

It is section of the warehouse, that groups storage bins based on warehouse activities. Activity areas can include putaway, picking and inventory. It consists of one or more storage bins.

Storage Bins can be defined using the following attributes:

● Aisle
● Stack
● Level

Talking about some operations like the Inbound delivery, Outbound delivery and Scrapping Process.

For Inbound Delivery:

The inbound delivery process starts with goods receipt and the arrival of the goods to the yard and transferring the goods to the final putaway in the stock. Transferring also can be for production and an outbound delivery.

Some actions can be done in the inbound delivery such as:

● Registering the delivery in the yard
● Unloading the delivery
● Canceling the “unloading of the delivery”
● Placing the delivery into stock (putaway)
● Canceling the “delivery putaway”
● Adjusting the delivery quantity to the quantity posted in the goods receipt (in case of overdelivery or under delivery.
● Adjusting the delivery quantity and the transferred quantity
In this case, the delivery quantity is adjusted to the quantity posted in the goods receipt.
The quantity in the inbound delivery notification is also adjusted and a new inbound
delivery is created.
● Posting a goods movement
● Canceling a goods movement
● Creating items
● Deleting items

For Outbound Delivery:

This process starts with the first planning activities for the outbound delivery and continues until the finished goods have been loaded and sent to the recipient.

Actions related to an Outbound Delivery:

● Creating an outbound delivery.
● Picking the delivery
● Canceling the “delivery picking”
● Loading the delivery
● Canceling the “delivery loading”
● Setting the status Leave Yard
● Adjusting the delivery quantity to the picked quantity
● Posting a goods movement
● Canceling a goods movement
● Creating items
● Deleting items

For Scrapping:

Scrapping within the warehouse, if a product is to be disposed of from the warehouse (for example, defective or obsolete product). Scrapping can be within a returns process. Scrapped products from different storage bins are moved to scrapping handling units. It is considered as an internal stock movements. The scrapped quantity is scrapped to a blocked stock. Goods issue can be used for the scrapping handling unit. EWM can perform the Goods Issue with generating the Outbound delivery document.

There are some another concepts in the EWM such as:

● Door
● Staging Area
● Work Center


For Door:

It is where the goods arrive at or leave the warehouse. To control putaway and stock removal processes in your warehouse, you can define doors and staging areas within a warehouse number. 

Door has various Functions:
● Inbound
● Outbound
● Inbound and outbound

For Staging Area:

Staging area is used to organize the flow of goods in the warehouse. They are located closed to the doors. They can be used for different purposes. For Goods receipt, which is Interim storage of unloaded goods until they are put away. Also for Goods issue, which is Interim storage of picked goods until they are loaded.

For Work Center:

It is the unit in the warehouse, where you perform activities such as packing, distributing
and weighing.

Work center can be used in the following processes:

● Packing for all general repacking transactions
● Deconsolidation: a feature that allows you to break down large inbound shipments into smaller units or individual items for more efficient storage and processing.
● Counting
● Quality Inspection

This article was an introduction for the organizational structure of the EWM from the highest level to the quant with more details. It also addressed main operations from a simple perspectives such as Inbound, Outbound and Scrapping. There are also three additional concepts in the EWM, which are door, staging area and work center.

entry.title: Crucial/key Setup needed for the Integration of TM TU-based Integration with SAP EWM
entry.content:
Only a few crucial, essential touch points will I go over; if you skip any of these, the system won't generate TU for you. It is important to ensure that you cover all necessary touch points in order to receive TU. Missing even one could result in the system not generating TU for you. Be sure to review and include all crucial touch points for optimal results.

 

  • Define EWM Integration Profile

  • Now Go to Freight Order and assign the EWM Integration Profile as well, make Documents Creation Relevance- E and Checkbox for Immediate processing.

  • Now Goto tcode- SPPFCADM For PPF activation from TM side

  • Now check for SAP EWM PPF activation in SPPFCADM

  • Go to /N/SCMTMS/LOC3 - Location master data and assign the Business partner as well as EWM Business System with EWM warehouse no.

  • Define Control Parameters for Forming Vehicles/Transportation Units

It is advised by SAP to create the vehicle/TU in EWM from TM send loading instructions, so please make sure that you create the same MTR here as well in EWM so that your TM MTR and EWM MTR should be in sync.

  • Tcode- /N/SCWM/PM_MTR

Test Script

Now if you Trigger the loading Instruction from FO Document, System will create TU in EWM OBD.

EWM ODO

 

Thanks & Regards,

Muhammad Ashraf

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Landing Page
entry.content:
Welcome to our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page

In short episodes we would like to give you an insight into the world of logistics with SAP Extended Warehouse Management.

We, that is Andreas Rupp and Matthias Nater. At SAP, we work as solution advisors for digital supply chain and we have many years of experience in consulting, development and solution advisory. 

 

Our SAP EWM Podcast is available in English and in German and you can find it the well known platforms Spotify, Amazon Music and Apple Podcasts! Enjoy Listening!

So far we have published the following Podcast Episodes. Use the links to visit the individual blog post for each Episode. 

Episode 22 - Customs Processing with SAP EWM - coming on 7th August 2024!
Episode 21 - Maintenance Logistics with SAP EWM
Episode 20 - Quality Management with SAP EWM 
Episode 19 - Wave Management with SAP EWM 
Episode 18 - Reporting & KPIs with SAP EWM 
Episode 17 - Batch Management with SAP EWM 
Episode 16 - Business Continuity for the Warehouse with SAP EWM
Episode 15 - Beautifully packed! Handling Unit- and Empties Management with SAP EWM 
Episode 14 - SAP EWM meets SAP Digital Manufacturing
Episode 13 - Efficient Picking with SAP EWM
Episode 12 - SAP EWM – Just Simple. The perfect solution for simple warehouses.
Episode 11 - Stay Future Ready with SAP EWM - Flexibility and Modularity
Episode 10 - SAP EWM in the cloud - where is actually my system?
Episode 09 - Let's produce - Production Logistics with SAP EWM
Episode 08 - Happy to see you again - Returns Management with SAP EWM
Episode 07 - Everything is moving - Automation with SAP EWM
Episode 06 - SAP Infotag Lager- und Transportmanagement 2021 (only in German)
Episode 05 - Mobile Working with SAP EWM
Episode 04 - SAP EWM on S/4HANA - Many Roads lead to Rome
Episode 03 - S/4HANA FPS1 2020 & SAP S/4HANA for manufacturing logistics
Episode 02 - Integrated or Decentralized Warehouse Management with SAP EWM - What Should I Do?
Episode 01 - History of SAP Extended Warehouse Management

You can find our former Podcast Notes also on our SAP EWM Podcast Notes Sway.

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 22 - Customs Processing with SAP EWM
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 22 – Customs Processing with SAP EWM.

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

Business requirement

In a globalized world where goods flows effortlessly cross borders and markets are interconnected around the world, efficiency along the supply chain is critical. While modern technologies and innovative logistics solutions such as SAP EWM and SAP TM optimize the storage and transportation of goods, a often underestimated process is indispensable! Customs processing.

For some, this step may seem like a bureaucratic stumbling block, in fact, it is the backbone of a smooth international transfer of goods.

In this Blog Post we take a look at SAP’s solutions that support and optimize these processes and how they interact with SAP Extended Warehouse Management.

Customs specific terms and processes

Duty-paid and duty-unpaid goods
Duty-paid goods are goods that have gone through all the necessary customs processing processes. This means that all necessary duties and taxes have been paid and the corresponding formalities have been completed so that the goods can be legally imported into the destination country or exported from the source country.

Typically, this includes steps such as…

  • The customs declaration, that is, the submission of the necessary documents and information about the goods to the customs authorities. This can be done electronically or in paper form.
  • Payment of duties and taxes: Depending on the nature and value of the goods and the customs tariffs of the destination country, customs duties, import turnover tax and possibly other duties must be paid.
  • The verification and inspections, for example the customs authorities, may carry out physical inspections of the goods to ensure that the information in the customs documents is correct and that the goods comply with national regulations.
  • The release of the goods, that is, Once all requirements have been met and all duties have been paid, the goods receive the customs clearance and can continue to be transported and finally used for the intended commercial or private purposes.
  • Finally, the documentation, that is, the creation and storage of all relevant documents that prove the customs processing process. This can be important for future audits or as part of the company's internal compliance policies.

In warehouse and transportation logistics, the management of these goods is crucial, as it ensures the on-time and smooth transport of goods and avoids potential delays or legal problems.

Duty-unpaid goods are goods for which these processes have not yet been completed.

Duty-unpaid goods must always be known to the customs authorities within the EU borders and there are various customs procedures that regulate the communication with the customs authorities.

Bonded warehouse / customs warehouse procedure
A bonded warehouse describes a warehouse that is specially authorized and monitored from customs authorities and in which a company can store duty-unpaid goods. It enables companies to import and store goods from abroad without having to pay customs duties, import turnover tax or other duties immediately or to be subject to import restrictions. The restrictions and duties are not due/effective until the goods are withdrawn from the customs warehouse and released for free circulation in the respective country.

Typical goods that can be stored in bonded warehouses include raw materials, components, consumer goods, and even vehicles. Companies in many industries, such as retail, manufacturing, and technology, use bonded warehouses to optimize their international supply chains and save costs.

Transit procedure
A transit procedure is a process that enables companies to transport duty-unpaid goods under customs supervision through one or more customs territories such as the EU without having to pay customs duties, taxes, or other duties immediately. NCTS - New Computerised Transit System - is such a transit procedure. A transit procedure must be completed at the destination of the goods, that is for example when the goods arrive at the dock in the warehouse. 

A transit procedure is required for example for the import of duty-unpaid goods from the United States that arrive in the EU via the port of Hamburg and are then transported to a warehouse in Walldorf. For this purpose, a transit procedure must be opened in Hamburg, for example, by the forwarding agent, and the procedure is then closed when the goods arrive at the warehouse in Walldorf. 

In the case of an export, the shipping of goods from Walldorf to the United States with the port of departure Genua (Italy) can be taken as an example. The goods – whether already duty-paid or duty-unpaid – must be transported through Switzerland and Italy – and a transit procedure is required which is opened by the shipper of the goods in Walldorf and it is closed by the port in Hamburg.

Temporary storage
Another procedure that often directly follows a transit procedure is the temporary storage. This is a customs status in which goods are placed before the final decision about their final usage from a customs perspective.

These goods are in a transitional phase in which they are under customs supervision, but they have not yet been subject to a final customs procedure such as release for free circulation, putaway into a a bonded warehouse or a further transport in the context of a new transit procedure.

Compliance Checks
Compliance checks include sanctioned party list checks and embargo checks as well as import and export controls for product-related compliance measures. These checks are important compliance measures in international trade that help companies to comply with legal and regulatory requirements and ensure that they do not violate trade restrictions, sanctions, or embargoes against individuals, companies or entire countries.

SAP Solutions to support and optimize international trade 

SAP Global Trade Services (GTS)
The SAP Global Trade Services application helps automate and streamline trade processes so you can control costs, reduce the risk of penalties and fines, and clear customs faster. With a single repository for all compliance master data and content, you can centralise global trade management.

SAP GTS Overview

 

SAP GTS checks current sanctioned party lists and ensures compliance with trade regulations, for example.

In addition, SAP GTS supports operational import and export processing by exchanging messages electronically with the relevant customs authorities or by managing customs warehouses. For this purpose, SAP GTS links the operational logistics processes running in SAP EWM or SAP TM with the systems of the customs authorities.

SAP GTS can also be used to manage trade preferences. This enables companies to utilize trade benefits provided by specific bilateral or multilateral trade agreements between countries or trade blocks. Trade preferences aim to promote international trade by offering more favourable trading conditions, such as lower or no customs duties and simplified customs procedures, for certain goods and services.

SAP GTS also supports the management of Intrastat declarations, for example, by providing statistical data in country-specific data formats. SAP GTS is the most powerful SAP solution to support customs procedures.

SAP S/4HANA International Trade
With SAP S/4HANA there is also an integrated functionality for foreign trade processing available - SAP S/4HANA International Trade. This component supports trade compliance in S/4HANA, handles Intrastat declarations and establishes the integration with SAP GTS if required.

SAP S/4HANA International Trade - Overview

 

SAP Watch List Screening
Customers who do not use SAP GTS but still want to perform sanctioned party list screening can use the SAP Cloud solution "SAP Watch List Screening". It performs appropriate checks and can be integrated via SAP S/4HANA International Trade Management. Open APIs are also available, for example, to make project-specific checks in other systems such as SAP EWM.

SAP Watch List Screening Overview

 

Customs processing with SAP EWM and SAP GTS

Transit Procedure in EWM managed warehouses
SAP EWM supports transit procedures in both goods receipt and goods issue. If the warehouse managed with SAP EWM is the recipient of goods for which a transit procedure is active, the warehouse usually requires the unloading permission from the customs authorities. With SAP EWM, we can ensure that no unloading takes place without loading permission and the process can be completely automated with SAP GTS. 

To do this, SAP EWM uses the previous document number – also known as the MRN (master reference number) – as a reference document in the inbound delivery. The previous document number is a number assigned by the customs authorities that, together with the previous document type, is used to identify the transport of goods. The previous document number can be found in the transportation papers of a truck and is entered manually in the SAP inbound delivery or it can also be sent by an advanced shipping notification. 

EWM settings for previous document types

EWM uses this number to recognize that a transit procedure is active and blocks the inbound delivery.

With the status “Registered in Yard”, EWM also informs SAP GTS about the inbound delivery with an active transit procedure. 

SAP GTS then communicates with the customs authorities by EDI and requests the unloading permission. When the release is received, SAP GTS sends a message to EWM, which unblocks the inbound delivery and allows unloading to start.

Transit procedure in an EWM managed warehouse in inbound

 

SAP EWM also supports transit procedures in goods issue, that is, if goods are to be loaded for which a transit procedure has been opened. This ensures that no goods are loaded and sent without loading permission from customs authorities. Remember our example of sending goods from Walldorf to the United States across the port of Genua in Italy.

The relevance of the outbound delivery can be defined using BAdI /SCWM/SHP_PROC_REL_SET in which, for example, all deliveries for a specific destination country like the united states or region are defined as relevant.

SAP EWM BadIs to influence SAP GTS integration

Relevant outbound deliveries are blocked by EWM for loading and goods issue. Picking and staging of the goods is possible and may be necessary if the customs authority wants to carry out a physical inspection of the goods. When the loading permission is received,the outbound delivery can then be released and loading can start.

Temporary storage in an EWM managed warehouse
SAP EWM can also be used to manage temporary storage, that is, the temporary storage of duty-unpaid goods until further use is decided – usually this can take up to 90 days.

Often, temporary storage starts immediately after the transit procedure is completed in goods receipt when the unloading permission has been received from SAP GTS and the goods receipt is posted in EWM. Therefore, the decision in which stock type goods receipt is posted also depends on the type of previous document number, which is the reference document type in EWM. 

In the case of temporary storage, the EWM settings for the previous document type define that this is relevant for the customs blocked stock.

Based on theses settings EWM posts the goods receipt of the goods to customs blocked stock – that is, a special stock type and then also reports this to SAP ERP. This means that the goods are also in customs blocked stock on storage location level in SAP ERP. SAP ERP then uses the goods receipt material document to inform the SAP GTS system about the stock in temporary storage.

In EWM, the goods can be moved internally and put away. Stock changes such as differences or scrapping, quality inspections, or value-added services, as well as loading and goods issue of the goods, are of course not allowed and are technically prevented by EWM.

After the employees in customs processing have decided on the further use of the goods and have made their decision also in SAP GTS, SAP GTS sends a message to EWM to release the stock for example. In EWM, a posting change from the customs blocked stock to the normal stock takes place on the basis of this message.

This process can also take place fully automatically after goods receipt posting in EWM if corresponding rules are defined in SAP GTS. In this case, the goods in EWM are only in the customs blocked stock for a very short time until communication via ERP to GTS and back to EWM is completed.

Temporary Storage in an EWM managed warehouse

 

Bonded Warehouse with SAP EWM
Bonded warehouses means that duty-unpaid goods can be stored in warehouses approved by the customs authorities. From an EWM point of view, the process is quite simple because there are normal stocks in EWM and the complete processing takes place using SAP GTS.

This means that EWM does not have any information about whether a stock in the customs warehouse is duty-paid or duty-unpaid. Each goods movement is reported to SAP GTS via SAP ERP. SAP GTS has its own inventory management for this purpose and knows the stocks accordingly. Therefore, the customs warehouse has no effect on the warehouse processes – except for scrapping. This requires the approval of the customs authorities. For example, unplanned scrapping of duty-unpaid stock. Without the permission of the customs authorities, customs duties and taxes would have to be paid here.

The scrapping process starts with a posting change request that can be created either directly in EWM or in ERP which is then distributed to EWM. The posting change request wants to transfer normal stock to scrapping stock. EWM creates this posting change request with status “Blocked” and EWM communicates the request directly to SAP GTS. SAP GTS communicates with the customs authorities and then grants approval to SAP EWM by transferring a scrapping reference number, which is saved in the EWM posting change request as reference number.

In EWM, the posting change request is then released and the posting change to scrapping stock can be performed. The transfer posting is then transferred to ERP and ERP informs SAP GTS about the transfer posting. In SAP GTS Inventory Management, the stock is then scrapped and SAP GTS sends an outbound delivery to SAP EWM via SAP ERP to post the goods issue for the scrapping stock.

Scrapping of stock in an EWM managed Bonded Warehouse

 

Compliance Checks in an EWM managed warehouse
SAP EWM also support the screening of sanctioned party lists, embargos, and other product-related export controls together with SAP GTS. 
For example, if an outbound delivery order is created in EWM, if goods issue is to be posted, or if certain fields are changed, EWM calls SAP GTS to perform the checks. In the case of a negative check, EWM sets the GTS check status to not OK and thereby prevents further process steps, such as the goods issue posting.

Invoice before goods issue in an EWM managed warehouse
With SAP EWM there is also the function to support invoice creation in SAP ERP before goods issue posting. This means: After successful picking and packing, EWM sends all information about the outbound delivery including packaging information and actual quantities to SAP ERP – long before the goods issue posting. 

Based on this information SAP ERP can then create the pro forma invoice, for example, which is transferred to SAP GTS as a preliminary document for the export declaration. This is usually required for exports e.g. from the customs territory of the EU to third countries.

Customs processing with SAP Transportation Management - short overview
SAP TM also has many integration points with SAP GTS. The central element in TM is the freight document, that is, the result of transportation planning.

Based on the freight document in TM, for example, Export declarations for export to third countries are made using SAP GTS or SAP TM can use SAP GTS to close a transit procedure for the transport of duty-unpaid goods in the customs territory. Compliance checks are also possible based on freight units or freight orders.

Customs processing with the new EWM-TM integration option "Advanced Shipping an Receiving"
SAP EWM and SAP TM offer various options to support customs processing processes. With Advanced Shipping and Receiving, however, SAP TM is put into the center of the E2E process and this also affects the customs processing. 

For example, compliance checks are no longer possible using delivery documents in SAP EWM or ERP. The checks take place centrally using the freight units and freight orders in TM – or based on ERP order documents, such as the purchase order or the sales order.

In addition, export declarations based on TM consignment orders and freight orders are submitted and no longer via ERP documents.

Also the unloading and loading permission which are required for a transit procedure are no longer requested by SAP EWM but by SAP TM. TM requests the permission with Advanced Shipping and Receiving for the consignments of a freight order from SAP GTS and then communicates them to EWM using special delivery status values.

Direct communication between EWM and GTS therefore no longer takes place for the transit procedures as well as for the compliance checks.

Only the release of the stock from temporary storage, that is, from customs blocked stock to normal stock, is
still communicated directly from SAP GTS to SAP EWM with Advanced Shipping and Receiving.

Conclusion: 
An implementation of Advanced Shipping and Receiving should also include a closer look at the SAP GTS integration.

Helpful Links: 

Blog Post SAP GTS vs. SAP S/4HANA International Trade

Connect SAP EWM with SAP GTS - Product Documentation

Customs Procedures with SAP EWM - Product Documentation

 

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 11 - Stay Future Ready with SAP EWM - Flexibility and Modularity
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 11 – Stay Future Ready with SAP EWM - Flexibility and Modularity

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy! 

SAP EWM is a standard software for warehouse management and intralogistics which combines the advantages of a standard solution with the possibilities of a tool box - from the beginning flexibility, modularity and extensibility of the solution were the cornerstones of the architecture!

And being a standard solution as well as a tool box is crucial for a Warehouse Management System because every warehouse is somehow unique. In addition, intralogistics is constantly changing because it always has to adapt to the requirements of its internal and external customers. And successful companies gain competitive advantages in the market by continuously optimizing their logistics.

A future-proof WMS must be able to support this constant change. Just as SAP EWM can do. 

SAP EWM is a standard solution!

First of all, it is important to understand that SAP EWM is a standard solution in which all available standard processes can be used via configuration. This means that the warehouse structure, multi-step processes, putaway and stock removal strategies, id point processing on conveyor systems, mobile dialogs and much more can be used out-of-the-box. And EWM can be implemented and adapted in a very flexible way to the company-specific requirements even via configuration and without a single line of coding. At SAP we call that customizing and master data. The following pictures should give an impression about the capabilities of SAP EWM. Link to official SAP EWM product landing page on sap.com. 

One Solution for all Warehouse Processes

 

What is SAP EWM Customizing?

The term customizing is often used by other WMS providers to describe adjustments of the WMS codeline. At SAP, however, customizing describes settings that can be made by the customer or implementation partner and then transported to the test and production systems. These customizing settings are warehouse number specific, so that different warehouses in one system can be configured completely different and independent from each other. 

Let’s make an example… You define a storage types in SAP EWM Customizing: How should the storage bin be found during putaway, do you want to manage handling units there - yes or no - and how should the stock behave when you mix different best before dates - is it allowed or not, do want to use the oldest one when you merge the stock? All these behaviours can be defined in SAP EWM with customizing and master data.

SAP EWM from a technical perspective - Master Data, Customizing and Codeline

SAP EWM provides an extremely large number of options to customize the system and, of course, best practice processes are available for all core processes in the warehouse. And most important... All possible standard processes, best practices and function-specific configuration options are well documented and the documentation is accessible for everybody. 

For simple warehouses, these configuration options are often perfectly sufficient. The SAP EWM Podcast Episode 12 covers this topic and explains why SAP EWM is also the best solution for simple warehouses.

Technical Frameworks in SAP EWM - hidden Champions!

When we talk about the flexibility and extensibility of SAP EWM technical frameworks in EWM are extremely important for the flexibility of the solution. SAP EWM uses these frameworks to provide standard functions itself and to enable customer-specific extensions of standard features. 

Frameworks in SAP EWM

There is for example the RF framework – RF stands for Radio Frequency. It is the foundation of the mobile working environment in EWM. The RF Framework provides mobile transactions for almost all processes in the warehouse which can be used out of the box in the EWM standard. These transactions can be customized and extended via the framework. And Customers can even build completely own mobile transaction on their own. In addition it is used to control the verification, to provide the right features to the right people and customers can have their own graphical mobile designs using SAP Screen Personas. 

Example: RF Framework for mobile Working in SAP EWM

The warehouse management monitor is also both a standard function and a toolbox. Both the reports for data selection (also called nodes) and the context-specific functions to control the processes (also called methods) can be adapted and expanded as desired.

The same applies to the integrated material flow System, EWM MFS. Extensive functions for telegram processing, for communication with PLCs and so on are available, but can also be extended very easily in the project. To make just one example. SAP EWM offers an ID-point processing with many standard checks and the creation of the putaway storage tasks. This can be extended via BAdIs or even used as a template for your own ID-point processing if required. Via configuration the own ID-point logic can be easily coupled with the telegram processing.

The Post Processing Framework is another central tool in EWM that is used in many functions. It is a little bit comparable to the message control in SAP ERP and it allows to trigger any kind of follow-up actions for the different objects in EWM. And there are many standard PPF actions that can be activated. For example the automatic printing for deliveries, warehouse orders, physical inventory documents or packages – so called Handling Units.  The automatic wave determination also runs via the PPF framework as well as automatic goods receipt postings or the automatic warehouse task creation for inbound deliveries. Customers can also easily create their own PPF actions and can implement any follow-up actions for the available EWM objects.

The condition technique is used to decide whether and how an action should be executed. This is another flexible tool to determine, based on rules, for example if a document should be printed and on which printer,  or to determine the right wave template for an outbound delivery item, or to find the right packaging specification for a customer order.

And, of course, the forms to be printed can be customized very easily too. It doesn’t matter if Smartforms or the newer Adobe Forms are used. Graphical tools such as Adobe LiveCycle Designer support the creation of customer-specific forms based on delivered templates in EWM. And this is even in paperless warehouses a very important function!

SAP EWM also re-uses the KPI framework of SAP S/4HANA. Therefore SAP EWM provides a lot Core Data Services with predefined KPIs which can easily be used to define own KPI Tiles in the system or which can also be re-used in the SAP Analytics Cloud for example for intuitive dashboards for operational reporting on live data of the productive EWM systems. 

In-App Extensibility with SAP EWM

But of course there are also requirements for which the many configuration options may not be sufficient. For example, a customer wants to influence the stock removal strategy very individually, which may require a few lines of coding. Or customers wants to store some additional information for EWM standard objects. Or customers want to adjust the look and feel of SAP EWM or... or... or... 

There are many use cases and reasons, why customers want to adopt or extend SAP EWM. And SAP EWM is made to support this - in a way that customers keep the upgrade secure of their EWM system! 

SAP EWM data model extensions 
Customers are of course able to extend the data model of SAP EWM - either with No-Code or Pro-Code tools. 

No-Code Extensibility of SAP EWM Outbound Delivery Order

For example the extension of the material master with customer-specific fields or the extension of outbound deliveries, sometimes in combination with the transfer of the required fields from ERP documents which also can require an extension of the standard SAP ERP to SAP EWM interface.

Pro-Code Extensibility of SAP EWM's data model

All EWM objects have predefined extension structures. Fields are automatically displayed in apps like the warehouse management monitor or similar. And interface extensions are also possible via predefined extension structures and BAdI implementations. 

And that leads us to a very important capability of SAP EWM - the availability of hundreds of Business Add-Ins so called BAdIs. 

What is a BAdI and why is it so powerful? 

Basically, a BAdI is predefined enhancement spot of the SAP standard software via which customers are enabled to influence the process or behavior of the software for example to influence the stock removal strategy. And best of all, these enhancements are absolutely upgrade secure. This means that after a release upgrade, these extensions will not only remain in the system, but will do exactly the same just as they did before the upgrade.

Illustration of a BAdI in SAP EWM

SAP EWM provides hundreds of these BAdIs. Well documented and often delivered with example implementations. That’s why consultants often call this extensibility approach the "extended EWM standard"!

And from my own experience, I would say this tool is the most important one when it comes to implementing customer-specific requirements in the system that go beyond the extensive configuration options of EWM.

Business Add-Ins in SAP EWM - well structured and documented

BAdIs also play an important role when we talk about a template and roll-out approach for SAP EWM and if we have multiple warehouses in the same SAP System which share some extension and which also need for some processes warehouse specific extensions. Therefore BAdIs can have multiple implementations and you can activate them by filter values. That enables our customers to re-use extensions or multiple warehouses and to differentiate warehouses if necessary - in one SAP EWM system of course! 

BAdIs - essential for template and roll-out approaches

User Interface personalization with SAP EWM

As a mature and grown solution SAP EWM provides different UI technologies like SAP Fiori, Web-GUI and ITS Mobile for the mobile working environment RF Framework. For all these different technologies SAP EWM provides No-Code/Low-Code tools to adapt the UIs. 

Fiori Apps can be adapted with the built-in Fiori tools for UI Adaption. 

Adapt Fiori Apps in SAP EWM

Web-GUI apps like the SAP EWM Warehouse Monitor can be personalized with SAP Screen Personas which allows to remove screen elements, to highlight screen elements or to rename EWM standard terms. 

Adapt Web-GUI Apps in SAP EWM e.g. Warehouse Monitor

SAP Screen Personas is also used for the personalization of the SAP EWM mobile working environment RF Frameworks which uses ITS Mobile to publish the screens on mobile devices. With the low-code capabilities of SAP Screen Personas SAP EWM customers can make the screens not only more intuitive but they can also automate flows in the RF Framework and you can for example use pictures to further improve the user experience. 

Adaption of RF Framework mobile screens with SAP Screen Personas

Classical In-App extensibility 

With SAP EWM running in private cloud deployment customers are of course still able to use the classical extensibility options like defining own data elements, structures or tables. And it is still possible to develop own reports, UIs or other technical objects within an SAP EWM system using the ABAP capabilities. However these kind of extensions are more and more shifted outside of the system known as Side-by-Side extensibility. 

Side-by-Side Extensibility with SAP EWM

With the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) customers can easily combine the core functions of EWM with innovative technologies from tour technology platform. SAP BTP offers for example no-code and pro-code tools to develop native apps or fiori apps but also tools like intelligent Robot Process Automation (iRPA) to automate processes in the system. In addition SAP BTP offers technologies like AI services which can leverage huge benefits also for warehouse logistics. 

For this reason SAP EWM provides many public APIs which can be used to extend the solution side-by-side with the capabilities of the SAP Business Technology Platform. 

Side-by-Side Extensibility with SAP EWM

 

Pre-built Add-Ons for SAP EWM - Don't reinvent the wheel... just check the SAP Store! 

Due to the long market presence and the wide and global distribution of SAP EWM there is even a market for SAP EWM extensions. The extensions are called Add-Ons, Consulting Solutions, Best Practises etc.. All of them have common idea. If there is a feature which is not covered by SAP EWM in standard it is very likely that there SAP partners who have implemented this feature already at one or many customers. And many of these partners develop based on this experience predefined extensions or complete services which can be used by SAP EWM customers to accelerate implementations or to close a gap which is not covered by SAP EWM in standard. 

Many of these pre-build extensions can be found in the SAP Store. Just search for extensions for SAP EWM!

SAP EWM extensions in the SAP Store

In the SAP Store there are extensions for SAP EWM which for example help to integrate an Autostore System, which enable the management of AMR fleets or which provides native apps for EWM based on public APIs. 

Conclusion

SAP EWM is standard warehouse management solution for all types of warehouses of a company. It doesn't matter if it is component warehouse in production, an intermediate warehouse for semi-finished or finished goods or a regional or global distribution center. It covers all sizes and complexities of warehouses and supports intralogistics processes in all industries. It's both an "out-of-the Box" WMS solution and also a powerful toolbox. 

Helpful Links: 

SAP EWM Support Content

SAP Onboarding Resource Center - Many helpful assets for a successful SAP EWM implementation

SAP Business Accelerator Hub - Explore SAP EWM public APIs

SAP Store - Explore pre-built SAP Add-Ons

SAP Discovery Center - Explore SAP BTP Services and Missions

SAP BAdI - Detailed Explanation 

SAP Learning Hub - Build and maintain your SAP EWM skills with predefined Learning Journeys

 

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 19 - Wave Management with SAP EWM
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 19 – Wave Management with SAP EWM.

You can also listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

Warehouse Logistics is not just about moving goods, but it’s more than ever about moving goods in a meaningful and efficient way.

Today it’s more important than ever to control and utilize employees and technology in the warehouse the best you can in order to reduce idle times. For this reason, we want to deep-dive into the topic of wave management with EWM... an extremely effective tool in warehouse logistics for optimizing warehouse processes.

Wave management with SAP EWM – let us surf the wave of efficiency.

Why make waves totally sense in a warehouse?
The idea of wave management in a warehouse is all about the coordinated processing of relevant orders. These orders - we call them warehouse requests in SAP EWM - can be outbound deliveries to customers, to subcontractors or other plants or warehouses, but also replenishment warehouse requests or staging requests for production. A wave collects the items of these warehouse requests into one pot in order to process them together.  

These waves – or more precise the items within the waves – then float together through the warehouse - from the picking area to packing to staging, so that the picked goods arrive at the destination area at the same time – and most important in-time.

Wave management in SAP EWM is even more powerful. SAP EWM is able to divide the individual items into different waves if, for example, due to the item quantity, EWM discovers that large and small quantity processing would make sense - e.g full pallet picking and piece or case picking. 

Example of quantity based wave determination 
Let's assume a customer has ordered quantity which we can fulfill best in the warehouse with a full pallet removal from the pallet storage and with picking the remaining quantity from the pick area. The associated lead times for picking are different and therefore it makes sense to divide this single item into different waves. One wave for the full pallet area and one wave for the picking area – and each of these waves has its own starting time but they are of course same expected completion time because the goods are shipped together. The decision in SAP EWM can already be taken very early at outbound delivery creation based on the rough bin determination which runs for each delivery item. The stock removal strategy is called and EWM recognizes that the single item will be picked from different areas based on the total item quantity.

Long story short: Wave management is a tool with which we can bundle warehouse request items very flexible and can then process them together in order to optimize outbound and replenishment processes. 

What are famous use cases to build Waves in SAP EWM? 

As mentioned above the source area for picking based on quantity classification is one example for building rules to reflect the different processing times. Other use cases for waves are...  

Wave creation per Truck or Container
A wave bundles all items which are planned to be loaded on the same truck or container.

Wave creation per Target Destination e.g. represented by the same Ship-to-Party or Route
This type of bundling is often used for small parts shipping with CEP service provider for example.

Wave creation to reflect capacity constraints of AGVs
This is an example of production warehouse where AGVs where used for the movement of components into production. Each AGV was able to carry 5 Boxes and each Box contained the components for one single production order. Waves were used to bundle up to 5 production staging request for common picking at goods to person picking station. 

These examples are only a small selection of use cases but they underline the different options waves can be used for.

Wave Management with SAP EWM - Overview

 

When we think about Wave Management with SAP EWM there are the folowing major questions: 

1) How do we build waves in SAP EWM?
2) How do we map the temporal dependencies of the waves in the system?
3) How can we influence the size and scope of a wave?
4) How do we determine the right wave template for an item? 

Let's give answers to these questions! 

1. How do we build waves in SAP EWM

SAP EWM supports manual and automatic wave creation. 

During manual wave creation, you select the warehouse request items that you want to bundle in a wave – manually – and you simply assign them to a wave. This can be a new wave or an existing wave which is then extended by the additional items. You can do this with the warehouse management monitor for example or you can use specific wave apps like the app “Maintain Wave”. 

To simplify the wave creation, EWM provides the so-called wave template. As the name implies, it serves as a template or building rule for wave creation and it defines many parameters of a wave in advance.

Parameters of wave templates:

Release Method
Defines whether a wave is released automatically, immediately, or manually later. Immediate release means that the wave release also happens immediately when the wave is created. Automatic release means that the wave is released at a certain point in time in the future. 

Wave Type and Wave Category 
The wave type simplifies monitoring because all waves of a wave type can be selected and monitored together for example "Small parts pick waves".
The wave category influences the rules of picking order creation – it serves as a filter for the warehouse order creation rules. This also makes absolutely sense, because small parts picking waves must form different picking orders compared to waves for the full-pallet area.

How long should a wave be open for the assigned of additional items
This parameter controls how long a wave is open for the assignment of further items. It is for example possible to add further items to a wave even after wave release. This makes absolutely sense if the warehouse receives orders very close to the planned goods issue time. In that case it can be, that the orders can be processed with a wave that has already been started and thus the items will leave the warehouse still in-time. A great example of warehouse optimization!

How should the wave deal with exceptions during picking
Items with missing physical stock in the intended source storage bin for picking due to inventory differences can for example simply remain in the wave and the wave can be released again later – manually or automatically. However, you can also remove these items from the original wave and assign them to another wave if alternative stock is available in other areas or you could assign them to an exceptional wave. And of course, you can also directly create a new warehouse task from an alternative storage bin – depending on how you want to handle these exceptions.

Rules and times for automatic wave release 
You can define for example not only the time for wave release but also a repeat interval for the release if exceptions occur during the wave release. And another very important setting of a wave template are the so-called wave template options. In SAP EWM, we use the wave template options to control the time dependencies of wave. 

Configuration of a Wave Template for Pick Cart Picking in SAP EWM

 2) How do we map the temporal dependencies of the waves in the system?

As mentioned above wave template options are used to define the temporal dependencies of waves in the warehouse. Let's discuss the most important parameters and we start at the end of the lifecycle of a wave! 

Wave Completion Date
This is the time or date at which all processing operations of a wave are to be completed. It is determined from the planned time at which a delivery item or a stock transfer item must be completed. 

Example
Let's assume it's Monday morning and we have an outbound delivery item that has to leave the warehouse on Tuesday at 6:00 o’clock in the evening to be in-time at our customer. For the destination area of the customer we have a wave template option defined with a wave completion time of 4 o’clock in the afternoon. In this example our outbound delivery item would in-time at the customer with a wave that has an wave-completion-time on Tuesday at 4 o’clock in the afternoon and we would create such a wave. If our outbound delivery item would have a planned goods issue time on Tuesday 2 o’clock in the afternoon, a wave which is completed on 4 o’clock on Tuesday would be too late. And therefore EWM would create a wave for Monday based on the same wave template option, that has a wave completion time on Monday, 4 o’clock in the afternoon to ensure an on-time delivery of our customer.

SAP EWM is  able to use the wave template options to schedule the outbound delivery items over the day in such a way that they are definitely completed on time, but are also not scheduled too early and thus block other and perhaps more important activities -always with the overall goal of sending the goods to customer in time. 

Find the right wave template option e.g. based on planned goods issue

 

Based on the Wave Completion Time, SAP EWM then determines further timestamps of a wave. 

Staging Completion Time, Picking Completion Time and Packing Completion Time 
These times define for all items of a wave when the corresponding process step like picking, packing or staging has to be completed. The Picking Completion Time is then also the baseline for EWM to calculate the Latest Starting Date of picking warehouse orders based on processing times for picking.

In addition, EWM determines the Wave Release Time and the CutoffTime of a wave based on the settings on the Wave Template Option. For the determination of all these times, EWM uses times and number of days defined in the wave template option that are recalculated as the lead time.

To stick to the example above, if our wave, which must be finished on Tuesday at 4:00 o’clock in the afternoon, is maintained for wave release 8:00 o’clock in the evening and 1 day before wave end, EWM would release the wave on Monday – that is, one day before Tuesday – at 8:00 o’clock in the evening. EWM uses the factory calendar in order to take into account also weekends and public holidays.

And a wave template can have many wave template options. By that EWM warehouses can define several options with different start and end times per day for each wave template. And that helps to divide the day into many small waves or time slices, which enables an even and efficient distribution of the workload over the day.

Configuration of a wave template option in SAP EWM

 

 3) How can we influence the size and scope of a wave?

This is done using the capacity profiles in the wave template option.

The capacity profiles can be used, for example, to define the maximum number of items in a wave, the maximum weight or the maximum volume of a wave.

 Capacity Profile configuration

 

4) How do we determine the right wave template? 

If we use automatic wave creation, the system uses the condition technique to find the appropriate template for a delivery item.  And based on this template EWM then creates a new wave based on the building rules of the wave template or it adds the delivery item to an already existing wave with the same building plan. Automatic Wave creation is done as a follow-up action for the outbound delivery or more general for an EWM Warehouse Request – technically, the Post Processing Framework is used.

Condition Records for EWM wave template determination

 Wave release then creates the picking orders. In this case, all items that are in a wave go together into the creation of the picking orders, whereby, for example, multi-order picking is possible. In addition, even the items of several waves can be transferred together into the creation of picking orders – this can be done in the warehouse management monitor by selecting the relevant waves and the warehouse monitor method “Wave Release".

What else... 

The wave in EWM is a real enabler for more efficient picking processes. You can find more details about efficient picking with EWM our SAP EWM podcast “Efficient Picking with SAP EWM”…

And by the way, before the actual wave release happens, the warehouse supervisors can also simulate the wave release. Wave simulation means that EWM runs 1:1 through the stock removal strategies and picking order creation without already creating orders finally in the system. However the simulation results are saved and they enable the warehouse supervisors to recognize very early whether there are stock issues or which workload is generated by the wave in the different areas. In this way, bottlenecks can be identified and corrected very early which helps to increase the on-time delivery rate.

An important technical feature is the parallelization of the wave release. In very large warehouses, in which there can of course also be very large waves, the release must nevertheless be processed fast. For this, the release of a large wave can be parallelized.

 Also two-step picking is controlled by wave management in SAP EWM. In two-step picking, the first step – the so-called withdrawal step – removes the total required quantity of a material across all items cumulated from the warehouse.  In the second step – the allocation step – the quantity is then distributed among the warehouse request items. The collection, which items should be processed together, and which quantity of a material is required, forms the wave. Accordingly, a two-step wave release takes place. During the first release, warehouse tasks are created without item reference for the total quantity of a material and during the second wave release, the warehouse tasks are then created with item reference for the allocation of the quantity.

With EWM you can even combine items in one wave for direct picking and for two step picking. EWM can also decide dynamically based on the quantity whether one-step or two-step picking should be used. This feature is called “Two-Step Picking Optimization” and the goal is, to minimize the number of picks in total. For example, an entire carton can be picked directly and does not have to be moved to the packaging work center. In this small example we would save at least one picking warehouse task.

The central tool for managing the waves is, of course, our warehouse management monitor. It can be used to lock and unlock waves for release, and to combine, delete, or create waves. Individual items can be split or reassigned. It is also possible to simulate the wave release with the warehouse monitor.

Central Wave Monitoring with SAP EWM warehouse monitor

 

If subsystems for picking are used, warehouse orders that have already been transferred to the subsystem can be released from the monitor at wave level. For that, EWM sends an IDoc to the sub system to release the processing of all warehouse orders belonging to the wave

Wave is a central and a very powerful tool in EWM which can be used out-of-the box by configuration. But it is also a technical framework which allows customer specific extensions if necessary. For example, the data model can be enhanced and customer-specific fields can be defined on wave header or wave item level.
And these fields can then be filled for example with BAdIs or own warehouse monitor methods.

SAP EWM BAdIs to influence wave management

In a project we mentioned earlier where waves were used to bundle production orders for AGVs, and the waves were assigned to goods-to-person work centers very early in the process. This requirement was realized by an additional work center field on wave header level which was maintained automatically when a work center had free capacity. 

BAdIs can of course also be used to influence wave determination, to incorporate own logic into capacity checks or to influence the calculation of wave timestamps and so on. A real framework – very flexible!

For the creation of wave related KPIs, different CDS views are available for the wave...  For more details about KPIs and measurement services for EWM please listen to EWM podcast about KPIs. 

Helpful links: 

SAP EWM Product Documentation for Wave Management

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: Supply Chain Challenges For The Back-To-School Season: How To Prepare Optimally
entry.content:
It may seem strange to already be thinking about the upcoming start of school, even though the school year isn't even over yet in many countries, and the holidays have only just begun or are still to come.

For many parents, schools and retailers, the back-to-school season starts in July at the latest. Retailers who are not prepared for the rush of prospective buyers could be left with disappointed customers. And as I have discovered, many parents of new first-graders in particular often start their preparations before Easter. If I had done so, then many school bag models would still be available! Which is why retailers who sell school bags and other school supplies should be prepared well in advance to meet demand.

It is interesting to note that although e-commerce has overtaken bricks-and-mortar retail in terms of sales in many areas, traditional retail remains the central point of contact for school supplies, school bags, stationery and gifts. According to eMarketer, 65.1% of these sales in the USA take place in bricks-and-mortar retail, with the share of e-commerce for school supplies rising from 31.5% in 2021 to 34.9% in 2024.

Challenges during the back-to-school season

The back-to-school season has traditionally become one of the most important sales periods of the year for retailers and manufacturers, alongside the Christmas period. However, with the expectation of increased sales and customer traffic, this season also brings a variety of complex challenges - from inventory management to on-time delivery, companies must plan and act strategically to meet the high demands.

This year's season will be affected by ongoing disruptions, seasonal fluctuations and labour shortages.

Global supply chain disruptions

Global supply chains continue to be disrupted by geopolitical tensions such as the Houthi rebel attacks in the Suez Canal and extreme climatic events such as the recent floods or water shortages in Europe. And it is precisely these disruptions that lead to delays and uncertainties in the procurement of goods. To reduce dependencies on specific regions or suppliers, companies should diversify their supplier base. Many companies are building local or regional supply chains in order to minimize delays and increase resilience.

Seasonal fluctuations and demand volatility

Companies need to plan strategically to meet the high demands of their customers. The back-to-school season is characterized by unpredictable fluctuations in demand and a wide range of products, making optimal inventory management difficult.

In order to meet the increased demand, especially for trend-based products that are subject to certain fashion trends or technological innovations, for example, retailers and manufacturers must organize their supply chain flexibly and create accurate forecasts. Predictive analytics tools can be used to forecast potential delays and take action to create a realistic demand forecast and plan production accordingly.

Warehousing and inventory management

In addition to optimized warehousing, efficient inventory management is crucial for the back-to-school season. Understocking can lead to shortages and missed sales opportunities, while overstocking can cause financial losses. Therefore, timely stock replenishment based on accurate demand forecasts is essential. In addition, implementing an effective inventory management system can help keep stock levels at an optimal level, maximizing efficiency.

Logistics and transport

Despite intensive planning by retailers and manufacturers, delivering products in time for the start of the season is crucial. During the summer period, logistics and transport capacities are often heavily overloaded as many skilled workers are on holiday. This can lead to bottlenecks and delays in freight and logistics services, which in turn leads to dissatisfied customers. Early planning of transport routes and cooperation with reliable partners is therefore essential in order to optimize delivery times and avoid bottlenecks.

Increased demand for labor

In order to cope with the surge in demand, companies hire seasonal workers, especially in the summer. Rapid familiarization with a company’s processes, tools and the existing workforce is very important so that these workers can be deployed quickly. However, companies can consider the benefits of using automation technologies for routine and repetitive tasks, for example, to reduce the need for labor and protect against labor shortages during peaks in demand. To retain existing employees and quickly integrate new employees, it is advisable to offer training and define clear communication of work processes to better manage seasonal demand and the challenges of the back-to-school season.

To be able to react quickly to changes in demand during the BTS season, comprehensive visibility of the entire supply chain and the use of real-time data is essential. Real-time data enables a better determination of arrival times in logistics and a more accurate prediction of supply and demand, which is particularly useful during the peak season.

This enables accurate planning of supply and demand, which is particularly useful during peak season.

Discover how you can build a resilient, flexible and customer-centric supply chain - download the latest report now!

entry.title: ASR Integration With SAP EWM - Outbound Delivery
entry.content:
Configuration

Make the Shipping Point Relevant for Advanced Shipping and Receiving

Tcode- /N/SCMTMS/LOC3

 Define Freight Unit Types

 

Freight Unit Building Rule- FUBR- Fiori App

Define Logistics Integration Profile

Define Freight Order Types

Activate BC Sets- Inbound and Outbound

I will add both Inbound and Outbound BC Sets which needs to be activated.

Inbound Delivery BC Set

Activate BC Set /SCWM/DLV_INBOUND_FO for EWM document type IFO

/SCWM/DLV_INBOUND_FO

Enable the item type IDFO for the use of serial numbers

In Customizing, choose SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Delivery – Warehouse Request → Quantity Offsetting → Define Quantity Offsetting Profiles

               Select the profile „/SCWM/INB_PRD_DLV_FO” and choose the view “Assignment of Quantity Roles” on the left side.

               For the quantity role “SN”, deselect the „Inactive“ checkbox.

 

Map Document Type from ERP System to EWM Document Type IFO

               DocTypeERP   EL

               Doc. Type         IFO

Map Item Type from ERP System to EWM Item Type IDFO

               DocTypeERP   EL

               ItmTpERP          ELN

               Doc. Type         IFO

               Item Type         IDFO

Now you need to do determination of WPT according to your Doc type and Item type

 

Activate BC Set /SCWM/DLV_INBOUND_PACK

If you want to automatically create packaging items for the packaging materials of HUs in the EWM inbound delivery, you need to activate this BC set. This BC set includes the EWM item type IPHU.

 

Automatically Generate Packaging Items for EWM Inbound Delivery

In Customizing, choose SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Inbound Delivery → Automatically Generate Packaging Items for Inbound Deliveries.

               Warehouse Number                 ZS01   

               Doc. Cat.                                        PDI

               Doc. Type                                       IFO

               Dlv. Item creation                       X

               Implicit GR                                    X

               Locking During Creation                        Blank

Open the view “Relevance of Packmaterials” and select the packaging type for the delivery item creation, for example, packing material type ‘PALT’.

Open the view „Determination of Delivery Item Type“, and enter the following data:

               Packaging Material Type         PALT

               Warehouse Number                 ZS01

               Doc. Cat.                                        PDI

               Doc. Type                                       IFO

               Item Type                                       IPHU

               Stock Type                                     F1

Define Delivery Item Category Determination for the Usage of Automatically Generated Packaging Items in EWM Inbound Delivery

In Customizing, choose Logistics Execution → Shipping → Deliveries → Define Item Category Determination in Deliveries.

               Delivery Type                                EL

               Item Category Group                VERP

               Usage                                              Blank

               ItemCat - HighLevItm                             Blank

               Default Item Category                            ELP

Add Transaction Code to Movement Type for the Usage of Automatically Generated Packaging Item in EWM Inbound Delivery

If you want to automatically create packaging items for the packaging materials of HUs in the EWM inbound delivery, you need to perform the following Customizing activity. This setting is necessary for the goods receipt reversal process

  1. In Customizing, choose Materials Management → Inventory Management and Physical Inventory → Movement Types → Copy, Change Movement types.
  2. Select the „Movement type“ checkbox.
  3. Enter the selection range from movement type 502 to movement type 502.
  4. Select the view “Allowed Transactions” and add the transaction code VL09.

Outbound Delivery BC Set

Activate BC Set /SCWM/DLV_OUTBOUND_FO for EWM Document Type OFO

  1. From the initial screen, enter transaction SCPR20 or /NSCPR20.
  2. Enter /SCWM/DLV_OUTBOUND_FO in the „BC SET“ field and choose “Activate BC Set” (F7). Check the BCS documentation and adapt the settings for your process. Enable the Item Type ODFO for the Use of Serial Numbers

If you use materials with a serial number requirement in EWM in the outbound process, you need to enable the item type ODFO for the use of serial numbers.

  1. In Customizing, choose SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Delivery – Warehouse Request → Quantity Offsetting → Define Quantity Offsetting Profiles.
  2. Select the profile /SCWM/OUT_PRD_DLV_FO and choose the view „Assignment of Quantity Roles“ on the left side.
  3. For the quantity role “SN”, deselect the “Inactive” checkbox. Define Process Profile for Document Header

You can enable the immediate quantity update to the LE delivery for quantity changes in the outbound delivery order, and immediate handling unit update to the LE delivery with the creation of the EWM outbound delivery.

In Customizing, choose SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Delivery – Warehouse Request → Process Management and Control → Define Process Profile for Document Header.

Enable immediate quantity update

  1. Select profile /SCWM/OUT_PRD_FO, and choose “‘Details”.
  2. Select the checkbox for ‘Quantity Update’.

Enable immediate handling unit update

  1. Select profile /SCWM/OUT_FD_FO and choose “Details”.
  2. Select the checkbox for ‘HU Update’.

Map Document Type from ERP System to EWM Document Type OFO

  1. In Customizing, choose SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Interfaces → ERP Integration → Delivery Processing → Map Document Types from ERP System to EWM.
  2. Map ERP document type LF to EWM document type OFO.

               DocTypeERP                  LF

               Doc. Type                        OFO

Map Item Type from ERP System to EWM Item Type ODFO

  1. In Customizing, choose SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Interfaces → ERP Integration → Delivery Processing → Map Item Types from ERP System to EWM.
  2. Map ERP document type LF, ERP item type TAN, and EWM document type OFO to EWM item type ODFO.

               DocTypeERP   LF

               ItmTpERP          TAN

               Doc. Type         OFO

               Item Type         ODFO

Determine the WPY based on your Doc Type

Maintain POD Relevance

  1. In Customizing, choose Logistics Execution → Shipping → Deliveries → Proof of Delivery → Set POD-Relevance Depending on Delivery Item Category.

               Item Category               TAN

               POD-relevant V (Copy POD relevance from preceding document)

               POD automatic            blank

Create Location – Loading/Unloading Point

Staging Area and Door Determination (Outbound)

/SCWM/STADET_OUT - Staging Area and Door Determination (Outbound)

Assignment of Unloading Points to Warehouse Doors

/n/scwm/door_scu

I have Completed the Configuration, Now Running and understanding the process is very much important.

Sine There will not be having any TU available the reversal process would be easy for the warehouse side, I will probably cover this topic in my upcoming blogs.

Testing/Running The Process

Create Sales order- It will create Freight Unit automatically.

 

 

Create Fright Ordre – based on Planning

 

Freight Order will be created based on your Planning Profile and Your Resource Master, Lane master, and Zone Master.

 

Since I am not using Tendering Process, I will assign the carrier Manually.

 

Now you create the Delivery from SAP TM- (0052 Control Key is required to create OD from SO)

As you can see from the screenshot below, "Send Loading Instruction" is disabled because the integration is ASR-based, and ASR does not support TU. For TU-based integration, please refer to my previous blog post. 

https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blogs-by-members/crucial-key-setup-needed-for-the-integration-of-tm-tu-based-integration/ba-p/13766871

 

Based on your SLoc in SO, the created Delivery will be distributed to EWM. I would like you to notice the difference between the OD and warehouse OD (if your EWM no range for OD differ, you can see that both nodes differ; this is the beauty of ASR, having both delivery with its differentiation).

Check the delivery in SAP EWM with Transportation Planning Type – F (EWM Level) and TM Controlled one will be E.

Actually, ASR support 2 levels, EWM controlled one and TM controlled one (the TM controlled one you can see only in S4 2023, I will add the path in the last fold of this blog)

Basically EWM controlled meaning, the OBD will not require any events from TM to start the warehouse process, where as TM controlled one require TM system to send you "Set in-Execution" until that time the OD will be in Block Status.

 

SAP Has given Specific App for ASR- Load or Unload Freight Orders, Here you can enter your Freight Order no and Warehouse no, and you can monitor your complete Warehouse activities.

 

 

Now you can process and complete the warehousing process (Picking, Packing but not Loading), you can go to the warehouse monitor and You need to do the ready for Shipping.

 By this time your Warehouse processing status will be- Completed. (Goto Status tab in FO).

You need to process the status - Set Ready for Transportation Execution

The Status will be in-Execution

Since the Transpiration Execution has been started, you need to set the process of FO In-Execution.

The Status will be changed to In-Execution.

Since the Truck Physically Arrived to your Warehouse for Loading, you need to set your FO for check-in, and for all these steps, relevant executions/events are getting triggered in background.

 The Evens will be below.

Your warehouse can do the loading by RF(Upon loading completion the OBD header status will be changed to Loading completion, and same you can see in the EWM OBD, as well as Load Unload Freight Order App).

Event

Now From The Load Unload Freight Order App, you should make it as loading completion.

The Loading Status will be change to Green(Completed)

Now you can post the GI from The Load Unload Freight Order Fiori App

Now all the process will be completed till GI.

 

There is some important point I would like to Add here.

1. If the events are not getting triggered, you need to raise it to SAP, there will be some notes which SAP will suggest to implement.

2. For TM Controlled ASR, in the SAP TM- Integration- You will find new Node there, where you can controlled the TM based ASR, and there are limitations are there, you need to read the Product guideline for that(Its only available from S4 2023).

In addition, I would like to thank my Mentor Mr. Ramakrishna Pantula for his guidance and My friend Mr. Tabrez Shaikh for this thorough ASR implementation.

Thanks & Regards,

Muhammad Ashraf

entry.title: Integration between SAP Yard Logistics and Dock Appointment Scheduling
entry.content:
I want to go over the operation of SAP Yard Logistics and Dock Appointment Scheduling in this blog post. To ensure that the warehouse runs efficiently and that the workload is distributed throughout the working day, it is crucial to schedule vehicle arrivals as well as the loading and unloading of the trucks. The carriers can enrol their transportation units for the yard and schedule time slots for un- and uploading with SAP Dock Appointment Scheduling, which is used to efficiently plan vehicle arrival in your warehouses. The planning document, or yard request (YR), is where the Yard Logistics process begins. I have manually prepared a yard request that is pertinent to the dock appointment here.

Configuration

 

Maintain Loading Appointment Relevance for Yard Order Type

 

Maintain Loading Appointment Relevance for Yard Order Type and TU Type

Control Automatic Yard Request Release

 

 Assign Document Categories for Dock Appointment Scheduling

Define Number Range Intervals for Appointment Numbers

Create Docking Location – Fiori App

 

 

Generating the time slot and establishing the warehouse door capacity at a given moment is the most crucial step.

When selecting a docking site, make sure to complete this setup; otherwise, you won't receive the appointment. Scroll down and select "Create Timeslots" in the same app.

The system will launch a new popup window where you'll need to specify when actual loading will take place in the warehouse, which is the warehouse that occasionally operates at capacity (the number of doors in your warehouse).
